speaker: interviewer
text: My name is my Myra Slotnick and I'm interviewing Edith Klar, June 7, 1996, in Las Vegas, Nevada. And this is tape number 3. Edith, I want you to tell me how your life evolved over the next several years when you came here with your parents after their basement apartment. What happened to you? Tell me about your education and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friends, and.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to grade school and it was a very difficult thing for me because teachers in those days did not help you if you had a problem. Their method of operation was just let her sit there, she'll catch on, which made me feel very rejected. I'm bringing in more emotion to this, but this is the way I felt. They had me sit there. And I had a couple of wonderful, wonderful friends that made it a point to come over and ask me, do you understand, and this is what she means, and this is the homework. And these things really stand out in my mind. The rest of the people were snobbish, I have to admit, in this town, and quite anti-Semitic, like I mentioned before. And I felt this. But there were some wonderful people who went out of their way, so kind. And one in particular used to come over and help. And I could always rely on my dad. Because I would go home and say, look, I didn't understand what she meant. When I was in Europe, if I didn't understand something, you got a stick over your head. The European way of teaching is really abusive, totally. Because, you know, there was no explaining again. You don't understand, you should understand. But coming into Indianapolis, it was different. There was no such physical abuse. It was abuse in a different way, where you didn't take the time. But I knew everything that was going on that the teacher did-- mathematics. Language was my problem. And I struggled through. And finally, I came to and I was able to-- the teacher decided that I was a person and not somebody from outer space because I came from Europe. I went through a lot during that period of time. It was-- part of it was from Europe, brought over, baggage. And a lot of it was I was trying to grow up. And then I had parents that were involved with their own terrible problems. I mean, their place, their basement apartment cost them $35 a month. That's all they could afford. I was lucky enough to go to camp through an organization, very wonderful organization, who helped refugee children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was some woman's league that I don't remember. But I remember some people really taking time and doing things for me. I remember one family-- excuse me-- taking me out for dinner, which I had never been out to dinner, never. And they had a couple of children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About my age. I was about 11, just maybe the end of my 11th year, almost 12. And I remember her being just so very kind and I thought, my god, she's taking me out to dinner, how lucky these children are, you know, parents that can do this, you know. And I had-- I had a wonderful, one wonderful friend that I met at camp. She was very good to me. And we remained friends for many years. And then I went on to high school and things were better. I could speak English. And I went into acting, some acting. I did some which I really liked very much. And that was a way of kind of venting and being somebody else, because I didn't like who I was at all, because that's how they made me feel, you know. And my self-esteem was just right in the ground, in the basement where I lived. [LAUGHS] And unfortunately, this basement faced my high school. And that was a great punishment. This boy had asked me out. I was just 14 years old. Of course, my parents didn't want me to go out, but I managed to persuade them. And I had to meet him on a porch, because there was a lovely lady who lived on the main floor and I asked her if she minded if I used her porch to pretend that I live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this young man, young kid, came to take me to a dance and I ushered him in quickly and out quickly. I didn't want him to know. And this young-- this lady was very kind. She allowed me to put a piano outside her door so that I could play piano and take lessons for $1 a lesson. I had somebody else who did this for me, very kindly.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were kind people in my life in Indiana, right through, here and there. But there also were very cruel people that I felt. And it was just, you know, people were popular. I was not popular. Like I said, I was not one of them, not one of them. And it was, it was rough, you know. And so that's why I went into dramatics. And I did-- I was quite active in that and did some work in a theater in Indianapolis. And I was told that I could-- should go to New York after graduation and try to break into theater. I had a girlfriend who was going to go with me. But instead, I had a visit from my cousin, Gerta, whom I lived with during the war, with her husband and child. And it was a wonderful reunion because we hadn't seen them since the war. She emigrated to Glasgow, Scotland. And she said, you know, she said, why don't you come to Toronto. She lived in Toronto. And she said, I'd like you to come and help me. She was pregnant at the time with another child. Her husband was studying the boards, because he had been a doctor in Scotland. He said, why don't you come to Toronto and help me and spend some time there. And I was just absolutely thrilled to do that. And my mother thought, this a good chance. She's going to be away from home for the first time and this will be good for her. And we arranged a visit, and I took the train to, to Toronto. And I was about to start college. I was just 17. And I was going to start college in September. And this was in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you going to start college?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to start Purdue University-- excuse me, Butler University there in Indianapolis. And it was all arranged. And I was in Toronto and I helped my cousin in every way that I could. And one day, she said to me, you know, Edie, I am just really upset because you're constantly working and you're helping me. And you're having-- you're not having any fun. So I said, oh, you know, I loved it. This was different. I loved her. She was vivacious, wonderful, bright, and just different life for me, you know. And it was a wonderful introduction to somebody else. And I kind of-- she was like a, she was like a model for me, you know. Anyway, I-- she said to me, you know, I want to arrange a blind date. And I said, well, OK, that's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever been on a date besides this boy was 14?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- not really, not really. And she says, you know-- well, I had, I had, a couple of times. There was one fellow who was studying to be a dentist, and a couple-- not a lot. I didn't date a lot. I didn't, wasn't like I said, I was not a popular girl. I was not. I was a very lonely girl. Anyway, she arranged for this blind date. And so he came in her house. He's all dressed, dressed up. And I was about to leave. The door was about to open. And there came a knock at the door. And this man was there. And my cousin Gerta just went absolutely out of her mind. She was so thrilled. She knew this man, Bernie, from Scotland. And her husband was very close with Bernie's brother, Jack. So, she says, oh, it was such a pleasure to see you. Oh, you know, I haven't seen you in so long. He came from Montreal, he was living in Montreal at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came to Toronto, which is about eight hours away by car, to visit her for a month. And he came with his parents. So you know, she said, Edie, I want you to meet this Bernie. And I said, oh well, nice meeting you and shook hands. I left with this blind date.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said to me, oh, don't worry, you know, you'll see him again. I said, well, OK, you know, fine. Anyway, she apparently had arranged for me to go to Niagara Falls the next day. She couldn't do very much, she was very tied to the house, and really didn't have much money to do anything with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she arranged for him to take me to Niagara Falls, to his parents' dismay, because his parents thought that he would take them around Toronto and drive him everywhere. But that didn't happen. But anyway, they did go with us to Niagara Falls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, after that, he took me out every night and every day for a week. And then it was time for him to go back to Montreal. It was time for me to go back to Indianapolis.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he called me on my birthday, which was in September-- and this was from August to September-- would I marry him. I was just 18. I had just become 18. On the phone he asked you to marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I was just-- and my mother was devastated. She was very, very unhappy about the whole thing. She was going to lose me and-- too young. And here I was going to start college. And so I knew things were happening and I didn't know what I was going to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the interim, I went to work for a rabbi in Indianapolis as his secretary. And this is the rabbi who perform the ceremony for our wedding. And so I was there. This transpired from August until March. And I got married in March. And what did Bernie do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernie was-- he drove a taxi. And my parents were not happy with that. But my husband did not have a lot of opportunities either. And that's what he did to make a living. His family had emigrated from Glasgow, Scotland, and it was also difficult for him. And he was searching for a profession. But my parents were devastated. They wanted a lawyer or a doctor for me. They had plans. But I was thrilled with him. He came to Indianapolis to spend a week with me. Then I went to Montreal to meet his-- the rest of his family, his brother and Jack and Betty his sister-in-law. And it was really lovely. I was received very well. And then I came back. And I got married, March 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long are you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm married 40 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a son, 34, who's a lovely kid, of course. But he is a very, very, very loving person. And he's in sales.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Murray, Murray, and-- Murray Lawrence. And he is, he's in sales, and he does very well. And he-- he spent, he spent a couple of years in college. And he's going back. He wants to go back. And my other son, Ron, and Debbie, my daughter-in-law, and Caitlin and my Austin, my grandkids, five and eight. And my other son is an attorney in Orange County. He's a public defender. He's been-- he's had quite a few grueling cases that I don't discuss much with him, but he's wonderful. Just really wonderful children. I'm very, very blessed. God rewarded me. I have a wonderful husband. And I felt very rewarded. And I did go to school for years. I went to college, but I never really got my degree. But I've always taken courses. And this was in-- we did move to Los Angeles in 1967, and were there for 28 years. And I went to-- to college. I went to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a secretary for Blue Cross originally. And then I moved on to Disneyland. And I was with Disneyland for 21 years. I was an executive secretary for one of the directors there. And it was a very, very challenging job, very challenging position, and very rewarding. I was very respected there. And it was wonderful. They were, everybody-- contrary to way I was treated in Europe and Indiana-- everybody was wonderful to me. I-- I worked with many, many people, many divisions of Disneyland, the studio, Florida. And I am very proud to say, not to brag, but I was very respected. And that made me-- that really made me feel wonderful because I really thought that I was pretty worthless. And that's how I was made to feel from the time I was born, practically, with the rejections around me. So it was really wonderful, it was-- it helped, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effect have your experiences had on the way you brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very gentle with my children and I was very careful with them. Everything I said, everything I did with those children, I made sure, I wanted them, and perhaps maybe too much at times, I wanted them so much to have the best that they could have, the best education, the best life. It had to be the best, nothing less would do. I didn't pressure too extreme, but I always encouraged. I felt I encouraged. And as grateful, they did go to school, they were never on drugs, they never drank, they were good kids. And they were normal kids. They argued amongst each other, they-- they were, you know. But I feel good about them. They're-- they're great. They're very respectful of us, and can't do enough for us. And it is just-- they're good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How have your experiences in the Holocaust influenced your life over the past 50 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's there all the time. I don't think it has left me. I don't think it will. I-- I tried very hard, as I said. To me, it's getting well. And I hope, before I leave this world, that I'm really well. I'm always working on it. I've taken many courses, psychology, and, and I think it all has to do with a healing process that has to take place. And I think my emotions, everything is a reflection of everything that happened years ago. It's permeated there in my heart. It just doesn't go away. It flashes, I get flashbacks continuously of my life. I can be taking a nice walk and having a ball and having good fun with my husband or, you know, friends, and it's there. It flashes. I hear soldiers, I can't take shouting. And that's not good. I can't take-- it bothers me. I cannot take crying. I cannot take a child crying. It devastates me. I cry when a child cries, no matter for what reason. I cannot take it. It-- it disturbs me deeply. And I see myself as a child all the time. I see myself as a little girl, crying all the time. I did a lot of crying, and I'm very prone to crying today. And I see myself as a poor little girl, and I am not. You know, it's just there. It's deja vu all the time, just things. But you know my husband has been so wonderful, has made me so happy. He's always tried. He knows, he understands, you know, he knows. And he's given me a whole different aspect of life. He's made me laugh, he's made me smile. I have a hard time laughing, you know. I can't sing, I can't. My husband said, in 40 years, I've never sung. I can't. I'm a singer, actually. I did it in school. But for some reason, I-- there's something that needs to be opened up in here. And it hasn't been. And it's happening, it will happen. And it's too bad. Some people-- I don't know if people can just snap out of it, or how long it takes. I don't believe that you can ever get rid of it. It's part of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this time? My father retired quite some time ago, and they've had a pretty good retirement. They've had a lot of friends, they lived in the valley. Unfortunately, they had a devastating experience in the Northridge earthquake. I happened to be in Fullerton at the time in my son's house, in my son Ron's house. And they were on the second floor of the apartment on Reseda in Northridge. And when the earthquake hit, of course, their balcony collapsed. And my father was tossed back and forth. And he had gone to the bathroom at that time of the night, or the early morning. It was about 4:32 or something. And he was knocked about. And of course, the shock was-- was horrendous. It was like, once again, what is this, you know. I'm sure it was deja vu for them. And they were rescued by a neighbor, a son, across the hall. And we found them wandering in the street in their nightwear. And they've never been the same. I don't think-- my dad has never been the same. He's 88 now. And I think it has done something terrible to him. And they've-- they lost a lot. Some of the furniture, some of their belongings were salvaged, but not everything. She's pining, she's mourning a lot of her things. And she relives this. And they're now in a home in Thousand Oaks. And it's a type of a home where they get their meals and they have entertainment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, I believe, is going to be 85 this month. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, why don't you introduce your husband to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Bernie.

speaker: interviewee
text: How are you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we've been married 40 years. And this man taught me how to laugh and taught my family how to laugh, because I didn't know how to-- to. I had no sense of humor when I met him. And he's got a great sense of humor, and he's been good to me. We've had very good years. We're a normal couple. And we-- I can't, I can't tell you how much he's done for me. I can't tell you enough of how he always is 500% for me and understands me, understands what I've gone through. And he's always there for me. He's there for me, all my life, for whatever I need. He's wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Las Vegas?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I get to Las Vegas?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you decide to come to Las Vegas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just-- I just really wanted to get out of Los Angeles, actually. Well, I figured I'm retired and I couldn't drive that-- those freeways anymore, because that's all I did for a living. And I said, and we figured it was a little bit more reasonable to live here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I happened to come here one day with my kids, and their friends came here. They come once a year to-- just the boys. And they asked me if I wanted to go, and I said fine. So one day-- I don't gamble that much, you know. And I left them, and I said-- I took the car, and somebody said something about Sun City. I can't remember who it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found out where it was, and I came out and saw it. And it was-- you know, it was just growing at that particular time. It looked very nice. And I came back and I told her about it. And she said, I'm not going to live in Las Vegas. [LAUGHTER] She says, I hate Las Vegas.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought of gambling halls and casinos. That's all I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's the only thing we ever knew was the Strip and that's all. A lot of people are the same way. So we came out for one weekend. And she saw this, she said, wow, this is-- this is not Las Vegas I know, you know. And that's more or less how.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how do you keep yourselves busy, what do you do now that you're retired, both of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I play golf quite a bit. And other than golf, I'm just, you know, really busy with other things. You know, you've always got something around the house and things to do. And at night time, oh, about three or four times a week, we go out to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was also going to UNLV for a while. And I possibly will go back and take courses. And I do line dancing and all the things that this community offers, whatever I can do. I do a lot of aerobics. And we have a good time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have some friends, and we go out, and sometimes go to casinos or the buffets, which isn't very good for you, I just know-- but.

speaker: interviewee
text: But besides-- I didn't quite continue-- but besides being a really wonderful husband, I have to say, he was an absolutely wonderful father to my kids, our kids. And I don't know of anybody that did as much for my children. And he was always there to take them away if I just uttered a sound that I was tired. And we worked a lot, but he was always-- I just want to be sure that I mentioned that, because that's what's in my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've never really-- I've never really said that to him, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, she has. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Just an opportunity to say so. But I think we have a good life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, it's pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is me. It's myself. And this was taken in Vienna, Austria in 1939. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was a special pose.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you? I was two years old, approximately. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just tell me when.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is of my father, Arthur, and my mother, Erna. And here I am in 1946 at the beach in Brussels, somewhere at the beach on the outskirts of Brussels and enjoying a day, something that we hadn't done very much before. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This was taken in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edie, go ahead and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding picture. And this was taken in 1956 in Indianapolis, Indiana, March 11, 1956. It's my husband Bernie and myself in one of the hotels in Indianapolis, Indiana. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, very good. Go ahead, please. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband Bernie and I are on a cruise on the Monarch of the Seas about three and a half years ago. We enjoyed a wonderful vacation on the ship. It was a lovely cruise, a brand new ship. And we had a wonderful time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son Murray. He's now 34 years old. And my husband Bernie. This was at a seminar for a national sanitary supply company. This was taken about, I believe about three years ago. And they worked for the same company at that time. And this was a seminar where they had presentations. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son Ron, and he's 38 years old, and my little granddaughter Caitlin and my grandson Austin. And this was actually taken about two years ago up in the mountains in Big Bear. They took a little vacation for a few days. And they were-- it looks like they were having fun there. So they wanted us to see how much fun they were having.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead, Edie.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my little granddaughter Caitlin. And she's five now. And she's in a karate class. So this is a picture from her karate class. And she's very proud of what she is and she's very tough. And she's adorable, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: They live in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm, they live in LA.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's five. Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Austin, my lovely grandson. And he's eight years old and he's also in karate and does very well in all sports. And this was just taken actually last year, actually about nine months ago, I believe. And he lives in Fullerton, California, where my children live. And he's fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter-in-law, Debbie. And my wonderful daughter-in-law who can't do enough for us. She's just always finding things that she can do for us in a kind way. So we really appreciate her. She's wonderful. This was taken last year. And I think she went to some kind of a makeup, makeover type of studio. And she wanted me to have that picture, which I think is just absolutely beautiful. And she's a wonderful mother, great mom. And she's lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're good.
